skill.name: telegram-automation
skill.description: Automate Telegram tasks via Rube MCP (Composio): send messages, manage chats, share photos/documents, and handle bot commands. Always search tools first for current schemas.
skill.summary: Automate Telegram messaging, file sharing, and bot management through Rube MCP integration
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 86 static analysis findings are false positives from code examples in markdown documentation. The file is a pure documentation skill for Telegram automation via Rube MCP service. No executable code, no credential exfiltration, no malicious intent detected.

content.user_title: Automate Telegram Messages with Claude Code
content.value_statement: Manually sending Telegram messages and managing bots takes time away from development work. This skill automates Telegram tasks through Rube MCP integration, letting you send messages, share files, and manage bot commands directly from Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Send text messages to Telegram chats and channels with HTML or Markdown formatting
Share photos and documents up to 50MB in Telegram chats
Retrieve chat information including member counts and administrator lists
Edit and delete messages sent by the bot within 48 hours
Forward messages between chats and retrieve chat history
Configure bot command menus and respond to inline button interactions
limitations: Requires Rube MCP server connection and active Telegram bot authentication
Bots cannot initiate conversations with users; users must start the conversation first
Message edits are limited to messages sent by the bot within the last 48 hours
Rate limits apply: 30 messages per second to groups, 20 per minute to individual users
Bot must be added to groups or channels as an administrator to perform certain actions

use_cases.title: Send Deployment Notifications
use_cases.description: Automatically notify your team about deployment status in a shared Telegram channel when CI/CD pipelines complete.

use_cases.title: Share Reports via Telegram
use_cases.description: Send daily PDF reports, screenshots, or log files to stakeholders through Telegram with captions explaining key metrics.

use_cases.title: Manage Community Bot
use_cases.description: Configure bot commands, answer callback queries from inline buttons, and moderate chat messages in community Telegram groups.

prompt_templates.title: Send a Simple Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Send a message to my Telegram channel @updates saying 'Deployment completed successfully'
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick notification after a successful deployment or task completion

prompt_templates.title: Send Formatted Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Send a formatted message to chat ID 123456789 using HTML with bold headers: 'Status Report All systems operational'
prompt_templates.scenario: Sending structured status updates with text formatting

prompt_templates.title: Share a Document with Caption
prompt_templates.prompt: Send the PDF report at https://example.com/report.pdf to @team-reports with caption 'Q4 Performance Report attached'
prompt_templates.scenario: Distributing files to team members with context

prompt_templates.title: Configure Bot Commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up my Telegram bot commands: /start for 'Begin using the bot', /help for 'Show available commands', /status for 'Check system status'
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial bot setup or updating command menu for users

output_examples.input: Send a message to @mychannel saying 'Hello World'
output_examples.output: Message sent successfully to chat @mychannel. Message ID: 123
Bot commands updated successfully. 3 commands are now available.

best_practices: Always verify the Rube MCP connection is active and authenticated with Telegram before running workflows
Test message formatting in a private chat first before sending to public channels or groups
Use HTML parse_mode for simpler formatting requirements, MarkdownV2 requires extensive character escaping
Split long messages over 4096 characters into multiple parts to avoid API errors
Handle rate limit responses (HTTP 429) by implementing exponential backoff between bulk operations
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode chat IDs or bot tokens in prompts; retrieve them dynamically from environment or configuration
Avoid sending the same message repeatedly without delays to prevent hitting Telegram rate limits
Do not assume the bot has admin permissions; check access rights before attempting administrative actions
Never include sensitive credentials or API keys in message text; use secure channels for credential sharing

faq.question: How do I get a Telegram Bot Token?
faq.answer: Message @BotFather on Telegram, use /newbot to create a bot, and copy the API token provided.

faq.question: Why does the bot fail to send messages to my group?
faq.answer: The bot must be added to the group as a member first. Invite @BotName to the group before attempting to send messages.

faq.question: What is the difference between HTML and MarkdownV2 formatting?
faq.answer: HTML is simpler with tags like <b> and <i>. MarkdownV2 uses * and _ but requires escaping special characters, making it more complex.

faq.question: How do I find my chat ID?
faq.answer: Use TELEGRAM_GET_UPDATES to retrieve recent messages, then extract the chat.id field. For public channels, use @channelname format.

faq.question: Can I edit messages sent by other users?
faq.answer: No, bots can only edit their own messages. To manage other messages, the bot needs administrator delete permissions.

faq.question: What happens if I hit Telegram rate limits?
faq.answer: The API returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests. Implement delays between messages and use exponential backoff to handle rate limit responses.
